meta.name: Jamie
meta.goal_note: MP = current goal pace: start 4:00 (5:41/km), shift toward 3:30 (4:59/km) as checkpoints allow. Football counts as a hard session — shuffle runs around matches.

Tempo: 2×15 min @ T, 3 min jog  +S&C A

MP reps: 5×1.5 km @ MP, 90s jog  +S&C A

34 km, last 12 km @ MP (final long — dress rehearsal)
MP reps: 5×1.5 km @ MP, 90s jog  +S&C A
MP tempo: 11 km w/ middle 6 km @ MP
RACE DAY — Tokyo Marathon · goal set by your build (4:00 · floor 4:30 · reach 3:30)

content.banner: ⚽ Football = a hard session. If a match falls on a Tue or Sat: don't also do a hard run that day — move the quality run to the next easy day, or let football BE that week's hard session and keep the runs easy. Never stack quality run + football + long run on 3 straight days; keep the day after a match easy. Max ~2 hard efforts/week (football counts) + the long run.

rows: Bulk of running — keep it TRULY easy
Race pace — MP blocks & long-run finishes
paces.note: Sessions say '@ MP' and '@ T' on purpose — run them at your CURRENT goal pace. Start at 4:00 (5:41/km); as checkpoints come in faster, shift MP toward 3:30 (4:59/km) and pull threshold with it.

checkpoints.intro: Run these honestly and set your race goal off the data. The Wk-24 half is the big one — 3:30 needs your half near 1:41; 4:00 needs ~1:55.
notes: This build pushes volume (peak ~82 km) so 3:30 stays achievable — volume is the biggest lever for marathon time.
Football & other sport count as training load — see the banner above. Don't double up hard efforts or stack three hard days.
One quality day (Tue) + MP long runs + the long run. Add a 2nd quality only if you're absorbing it cleanly.
If 82 km proves too much with football, ~68 km still delivers a strong 4:00.

strength.intro: 2×/week, 20–35 min. Injury-proof tendons/hips + improve running economy. TIMING: after runs, on your hard day (Tue = Session A) + one easy day (Fri = Session B). Never heavy legs before a long run or a match. Form first, load second.

blocks.title: Foundation · Weeks 1–18
blocks.note: Build tissue tolerance, bodyweight → light load. 2×/week.
rows: Full depth, controlled — base leg strength
Core without arching the lower back

blocks.title: Strength & Power · Weeks 19–30
blocks.note: Heavier + a little spring for economy & late-race power. 2×/week.
rows: Power — land soft; skip if legs cooked

blocks.title: Taper · Weeks 31–33
rows: Bodyweight circuit (squat, calf raise, bridge, plank)
Keep the pattern, add zero fatigue
Stop ALL strength 5–7 days before race day

blocks.title: Daily prehab · 5 min, most days
blocks.note: Little-and-often — keeps you on the road while mileage climbs.

fuel.intro: You can't out-train poor fuelling, sleep or recovery — it matters more with 80 km weeks + football. Fuel the work, protect recovery, be smart about alcohol, and know when NOT to run.

sections.title: Daily diet · fuel the work
rows: Your main running fuel — don't fear it. More around long/quality days.
~1.6–2.0 g/kg/day across meals — repairs muscle & tendon as you build.
As mileage rises, under-eating causes injury, illness and burnout. Eat enough.
Veg, fruit, whole grains, quality protein, healthy fats. Check iron if chronically flat.

sections.title: Before running · what & when
rows: Fine fasted or light — coffee + banana.
Fuel first: a carb meal 2–3 h before (porridge + banana, toast + eggs), low fat/fibre.
Quick top-up if needed: banana, toast + jam, or a gel.
Caffeine is a legit, legal boost before hard sessions and races.

sections.title: During & after · refuel & recover
rows: 40–60 g carbs/hr (gels/drink) + fluids. Start early, don't wait to feel empty.
After long/quality runs: carbs + 20–30 g protein within ~60 min.
Replace ~1.25–1.5× fluid lost; add salt if you're a salty/heavy sweater.

sections.title: Alcohol · the honest version
rows: Wrecks deep sleep, dehydrates, blunts recovery, raises next-day injury risk.
If you drink, do it after a hard session / before a rest day — never before a long run, quality day or race.
Minimise in the last 2 weeks; zero in race week.

sections.title: Should I run today? · quick guide
rows: Skip quality & long runs (dehydration + poor coordination = injury). Full rest or an easy jog only if mild and rehydrated first. Reschedule the key session; don't cram it back.
Pain that changes your stride or worsens as you run → STOP. 2–3 easy/off days now beats weeks off later. Sharp/one-sided/joint pain → rest & assess. Cross-train pain-free to keep fitness.
Not better in a few days, or worse? See a physio early — don't 'test it' with a hard run.
Above the neck (runny nose, mild throat) → easy run usually OK. Below the neck (chest, fever, aches) → REST. NEVER run with a fever.
Heavy legs, rising resting HR, poor sleep = under-recovery. Take the easy day easy or swap to rest. Missing one session to absorb training is smart.
If a match or tournament ate your legs, downgrade the next run to easy/recovery and protect the long run. Two hard efforts a week total — matches included.

tips: Volume is the big lever
Weekly mileage moves your marathon time more than anything — that's why the peak is ~82 km. But it only counts if you stay healthy and consistent.
Football is a hard session
Treat every match as one of your two weekly hard efforts. No hard run the same day; keep the day after a match easy.
Shuffle, don't skip

Match Tuesday? Move the quality run to Wednesday and slide the easy days along. Match Saturday? Keep Sunday's long run easy, or push it to Monday if legs are cooked. Protect the long run; don't stack three hard days.
Easy means EASY
Most running should be conversational — even slower than feels 'proper'. That's where the engine is built.
The niggle rule
Pain that changes your gait or worsens = stop. 2–3 easy/off days early beats 3 weeks off later.
Guard the long run
The single most important marathon session — the last thing to sacrifice when the week gets messy.
Sleep is the #1 recovery tool
7–9 h/night. Higher volume needs more recovery, not less.
Fuel the long ones
Past 90 min take 40–60 g carbs/hr and rehearse your race-day breakfast on long runs.
Rotate 2 pairs of shoes
Cuts injury risk, extends shoe life (~500–800 km). Buy race shoes by the tune-up and run 3–4 sessions in them.
Warm up the hard day
10–15 min easy + a few strides before any tempo/threshold/VO2 work.
Respect cut-back weeks
The down weeks are when fitness consolidates — don't 'top them up'.
Calibrate at the half
The tune-up half sets your real race pace. Race the pace you EARNED, not the dream.
Start the race easy
First few km should feel too easy. Hold back — the marathon starts at 30 km. Even pacing wins.
Race-day fuel clock
Gel every 30–40 min from ~45 min in, with water. Don't wait until you're empty.
Tokyo in early March
~8–12°C, flat, fast — a great course. Dress for ~+10°C warmer than standing; throwaway layer at the start.
Nothing new two weeks out
No new shoes, foods or bonus sessions. Lock the routine and trust the work.
Log everything
Fill it in after each run — it keeps you honest and shows patterns before they become problems.
